subject: eddy-lazzarin

sectors_excited.name: Zero-knowledge proofs (ZK rollups + privacy)
sectors_excited.why: ZK is the load-bearing primitive for both scaling (validity-proof rollups as Ethereum's eventual compute layer) and privacy-preserving applications. Decades-old theory, but research has accelerated radically because of web3 — and the tooling is finally crossing from theory into practice.

citations.quote: research has accelerated radically as a result of web3.
instead of having to calculate it all yourself, if there was a way to construct a proof that it was done correctly, so a very short, quick and dirty way to verify that everything was done

sectors_excited.name: Fully on-chain games
sectors_excited.why: Games are serious incentive systems, not a hobby. As fully on-chain architectures mature they'll fill the niche the great programmer-games did — emergent, player-owned economies where items and mechanics live on a shared-state computer rather than a company's server.

citations.quote: I believe fully on-chain games will fill a similar niche in coming years.

sectors_excited.name: Decentralized identity & auth
sectors_excited.why: Unbundling login and rebuilding it from first principles on public/private keypairs. DIDs and Sign-in with Ethereum let people authenticate without ceding data to intermediaries — an improvement for web2 services too, not just web3.

citations.quote: make identity more private, secure, and programmable.
potentially replacing username/password authentication and giving people complete control of their data.

sectors_excited.name: AI + crypto intersection
sectors_excited.why: My freshest thesis. AI is how machines think; crypto is how machines own. Put together you get programs that can act and transact on their own — sovereign, autonomous applications. We have only ~8 years of thought here since Ethereum, so the design space is wide open.

citations.quote: AI + crypto = how machines can think on their own
We only have 14 years of thought (maybe really 8 since Ethereum) around sovereign programs / autonomous applications.

sectors_excited.name: Decentralized social networks
sectors_excited.why: We learned the hard way that building on centralized social platforms gets you rugged — cut off, replaced, blocked from monetizing once they have the network effects to break the promise. Neutral, credibly-non-extractive protocols are the platform we always wanted.

citations.quote: Farcaster is the neutral platform we wanted all along.
When they have the network effects to break that promise without risk, they do so at the first opportunity.

sectors_excited.name: NFTs as programmable objects & creator economy
sectors_excited.why: NFTs aren't JPEGs — they're programmable, can hold mutable state, and grant real digital property rights. The economics for creators are structurally better: web3 marketplaces take a fraction of what web2 platforms extract.

citations.quote: NFTs are programmable.
OpenSea keeps 2.5%. On Instagram, creators make nothing

sectors_excited.name: DeFi / new on-chain financial primitives
sectors_excited.why: DeFi is composable and uniquely analyzable — primitives can be remixed like lego bricks. I'm particularly interested in fixed-rate / fixed-income building blocks, which are powerful collateral types just as they are in traditional finance, and in the still-underreported split between supply-side and protocol fees.

citations.quote: Fixed rate assets seem like a powerful collateral type for lending protocols, as they already are in trad finance.
DeFi is deeply different from traditional finance because there's so much more to analyze.

sectors_concerned.name: Centralized platform gatekeeping (Apple's 30% / mobile distribution)
sectors_concerned.why: The platform tax on innovation is radically underestimated. Try to offer a native crypto experience on mobile and Apple will cripple you, block you, or take its cut. This is a direct, underrated drag on what builders can ship — the browser remains the most important escape hatch for a free and open web.

citations.quote: The effects of Apple's 30% fee on innovation are radically underestimated
Apple will cripple you, block you, or take your lunch if you try to offer a native experience.

sectors_concerned.name: Centralized exchanges & sham 'proof of reserves'
sectors_concerned.why: After this year's blowups, exchanges are scrambling to show 'proof of reserves' with methods that don't actually prove anything — no shared Merkle tree, no handling of negative balances/liabilities, no evidence they control the addresses. The whole point of crypto is direct, verifiable control of assets; opaque custodians are the opposite of that.

citations.quote: Binance's system is useless and easily exploited by them.
Negative balances are a known fatal flaw of the "proof of reserves" method Binance shared

sectors_concerned.name: 'Tokenomics' pie-chart projects (tokens without protocols)
sectors_concerned.why: Consultants who 'work with you' to pick token allocations are a waste of time — a made-up pie chart is not real work. A protocol needs a clear goal; launching a token isn't a goal. Allocation is maybe 5% of the work at the end of a real design process, and a lot of capital is flowing toward the inverse of that.

citations.quote: A made up pie chart is not real work.
A protocol needs a clear goal. Launching a token isn't a goal.

tokens_excited.name: ETH
tokens_excited.why: The clear leader by the metric that matters most — developer mindshare — and willingness to pay $15m+/day in fees to use it. The Merge executed cleanly, which de-risks the long-planned roadmap toward proof-of-stake and a ZK-rollup-centric future. Not a price call; an assessment of fundamentals and momentum.

citations.quote: Other layer 1 blockchains have grown rapidly over the last year, but @ethereum is the clear leader.
Congratulations and well done to the ETH 2 researchers, client teams, core devs, and all other contributors.

tokens_excited.name: Protocol 'risk tokens' (MKR, AAVE, COMP)
tokens_excited.why: Underrated because the discourse fixates on 'governance' and ignores their real function: bearing risk and underwriting the protocol, with actual fundamentals and access to cash flows. Tokens are the native asset class of information networks — these are among the few with a legible cash-flow/risk story.

citations.quote: the role of MKR and AAVE/COMP as the risk token that underwrites the protocol often seems to take a back seat rhetorically to "governance" unfortunately
Tokens can be designed to have access to cash flows.

tokens_concerned.name: Centralized-exchange tokens (e.g. BNB)
tokens_concerned.why: My concern is the issuer, not a price target: the entities behind these tokens are the same ones publishing unverifiable 'proof of reserves.' When a custodian can't credibly demonstrate solvency or even control of its addresses, the token sitting on top of that opacity inherits the risk.

citations.quote: Binance's system is useless and easily exploited by them.

risk_regime.why: Constructive on a long horizon, and deliberately so during the downturn — not a leveraged short-term bet. The price-innovation cycle says winter is exactly when builders plant the seeds of the next cycle; founders who swore off tech after the dotcom crash missed the decade's best opportunities. I fly by instruments (fundamentals, tech, talent flows), not the sentiment herd. Skeptical in analysis, optimistic in action. It's still early.

citations.quote: despite financial turbulence, things are proceeding according to plan
there is an underlying order to the chaos in crypto markets
It's still early.

notes: I'm an engineer/builder, not a trader — these are mechanism-design and fundamentals views, not investment advice or price calls. My excitement clusters where new primitives unlock things that were previously impossible (ZK, on-chain games, identity, AI×crypto); my concerns cluster around extractive gatekeepers and opaque intermediaries (Apple's tax, CEX custody) and around the noise of tokens launched without real protocol design. Posture: keep building.
